title: Recursion
description: Recursion is the foundation of divide-and-conquer, tree traversal, and backtracking. Master base cases, HashMap memoization to avoid recomputation, backtracking with choose/unchoose, and converting recursive solutions to iterative ones.

sections.title: Base case and recursive structure
sections.explanation: Every recursive function needs a **base case** (when to stop) and a **recursive case** (how to reduce the problem). In Rust, the function signature and return type must be explicit. Classic examples: factorial (base: n <= 1 returns 1) and Fibonacci (base: n <= 1 returns n).

exercise.prompt: Write a recursive `sum_to` function that returns the sum 1 + 2 + ... + n. Base case: n <= 0 returns 0. Test with n = 10 (should print 55).

validation.message: Base case returns 0, recursive case adds n to sum_to(n-1)

exercise.hint: Base case returns 0. Recursive step: n + sum_to(n - 1).

sections.title: HashMap memoization
sections.explanation: Naive recursion (like Fibonacci) has exponential time complexity due to overlapping subproblems. Use a **HashMap** as a memo cache: before computing, check if the result is cached. If yes, return it. If not, compute, store, and return. This brings Fibonacci from O(2^n) to O(n).

exercise.prompt: Implement memoized Fibonacci using a HashMap. Compute fib(40) and fib(45). Print both results.

validation.message: Check memo with get(), store result with insert()

exercise.hint: Store the computed result: memo.insert(n, result).

sections.title: Backtracking (subsets)
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores all candidates with the **choose-explore-unchoose** pattern. For subsets: at each element, decide to include or exclude it. Push the element, recurse, pop (backtrack), then recurse without it. This drives subset, permutation, and combination problems.

exercise.prompt: Complete the subsets function that generates all subsets of [1, 2, 3]. Print the total count (should be 8).

validation.message: Push to choose, recurse, pop to unchoose, clone to save current subset

exercise.hint: Call path.pop() after the recursive call to undo the choice (backtrack).

sections.title: Converting recursion to iteration
sections.explanation: Some recursive solutions can cause stack overflow for large inputs. Convert to iterative using an explicit stack or tabulation (bottom-up DP). For Fibonacci: build the answer from the bottom up with a simple loop and two variables, achieving O(n) time and O(1) space.

exercise.prompt: Convert Fibonacci to an iterative solution using two variables. Compute fib(10) and fib(50). Print both.

validation.message: Shift variables: temp = a+b, a = b, b = temp

exercise.hint: After computing temp = a + b, shift: a becomes b, and b becomes temp.
